replacements.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct

Orientation. This reading is the map of the whole ethics topic: the Code's six components, the seven Standards and their subsections, and the Professional Conduct Program (PCP) that enforces them. Item sets rarely test this reading in isolation — it supplies the vocabulary the guidance readings and the application reading then exercise — but the PCP mechanics and the exact architecture of the Standards are directly testable and cheap points.

1. The enforcement machinery (LOS a)
The Professional Conduct Program operates under the Bylaws and Rules of Procedure, overseen by the Disciplinary Review Committee (a volunteer body of members). Everything rests on self-disclosure plus surveillance: inquiries can begin from a member's own annual Professional Conduct Statement disclosures, written complaints from any party, media or public information suggesting misconduct, exam-administration reports (proctors, analytics on answer patterns), or CFA Institute's own monitoring.

Once an inquiry opens, Professional Conduct staff investigate: they request a written explanation from the member or candidate, may interview the member, complainants, and third parties, and collect relevant documents. Staff then propose an outcome. Three paths: (1) no disciplinary action; (2) a cautionary letter — a private communication, not a public sanction; (3) a disciplinary sanction. If the member rejects a proposed sanction, the matter goes to a hearing panel of members, whose decision is final within the program.

Sanctions run from public censure through suspension of membership and the right to use the designation, up to revocation. Candidates can be suspended or prohibited from further participation in the exam program. The exam-relevant nuances: a cautionary letter is private; sanctions are public; members are obligated to cooperate with an investigation — refusing to cooperate can itself be treated as conduct violating the Code; and the PCP cannot fine or imprison anyone — it governs membership and the designation only.

2. The Code of Ethics — six commitments (LOS b)
Members and candidates commit, in substance, to: act with integrity, competence, diligence, and respect, in an ethical manner with the public, clients, prospects, employers, employees, and colleagues; place the integrity of the profession and the interests of clients above their own interests; use reasonable care and independent professional judgment in all professional activities; practice and encourage others to practice professionally and ethically in a way that credits themselves and the profession; promote the integrity and viability of global capital markets for society's ultimate benefit; and maintain and improve their own professional competence while striving to maintain that of other professionals.

Two hierarchy points recur on exams: client interests sit above employer interests, and market integrity / professional integrity sit above both when they conflict; and the Code binds conduct even where no law does — the Standards are frequently stricter than local law.

3. The seven Standards and their subsections (LOS b)
Standard I — Professionalism: (A) Knowledge of the Law — comply with the strictest applicable law, regulation, or the Code, and dissociate from violations; (B) Independence and Objectivity — do not offer, solicit, or accept anything that could reasonably be expected to compromise independence; (C) Misrepresentation — no knowing misrepresentations, including guarantees of performance and plagiarism; (D) Misconduct — no dishonesty, fraud, or deceit, and no conduct reflecting adversely on professional reputation, integrity, or competence; (E) Competence — maintain and act with the competence the professional role requires.

Standard II — Integrity of Capital Markets: (A) Material Nonpublic Information — do not act or cause others to act on MNPI (the mosaic of nonmaterial nonpublic plus public information is permitted); (B) Market Manipulation — no practices that distort prices or artificially inflate volume with intent to mislead, whether transaction-based or information-based.

Standard III — Duties to Clients: (A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care — client interests first, prudent-investor care, know who the actual client is (for a plan, the beneficiaries); (B) Fair Dealing — deal fairly and objectively with all clients in recommendations and investment action; fair does not mean identical; (C) Suitability — in advisory relationships: reasonable inquiry, documented IPS reviewed regularly, judge suitability in the TOTAL portfolio context; when managing to a mandate, manage to the mandate; (D) Performance Presentation — fair, accurate, and complete performance communication; (E) Preservation of Confidentiality — keep current, former, and prospective client information confidential unless it concerns illegal activity, disclosure is legally required, or the client permits it.

Standard IV — Duties to Employers: (A) Loyalty — no harm to the employer, no misappropriation of skill-embodied property (client lists, models, records); independent practice that competes requires consent; departing employees may take general knowledge and skill, not employer property; (B) Additional Compensation Arrangements — no gifts or compensation that compete with or might create a conflict with the employer's interest without WRITTEN consent from all parties involved; (C) Responsibilities of Supervisors — make reasonable efforts to detect and prevent violations by anyone subject to supervision or authority; inadequate compliance systems must be declined or escalated.

Standard V — Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions: (A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis — thorough, diligent analysis with a reasonable and adequate basis, including due diligence on third-party research and quantitative tools; (B) Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients — disclose the investment process and its changes, disclose significant limitations and risks, distinguish FACT from OPINION, and communicate in a fair format; (C) Record Retention — develop and maintain records supporting analysis and actions; records generated at work belong to the employer; absent regulation, seven years is the recommended holding period.

Standard VI — Conflicts of Interest: (A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — avoid where possible; otherwise full, fair, prominent, plain-language disclosure to clients and employer (older materials label this Disclosure of Conflicts); (B) Priority of Transactions — client and employer transactions take precedence over transactions in which a member is a beneficial owner; (C) Referral Fees — disclose to employer, clients, and prospects any compensation or benefit received for, or paid for, referrals, before entering the arrangement.

Standard VII — Responsibilities as a CFA Institute Member or Candidate: (A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs — no conduct compromising the integrity or security of CFA Institute programs (exam content confidentiality, no cheating, honest PCS); expressing opinions about the program is permitted, revealing exam content is not; (B) Reference to CFA Institute, the Designation, and the Program — no exaggerating the meaning or implications of membership or candidacy; the designation is used as an adjective, never a noun; candidacy may be stated only while actually registered for the next exam, and passing levels may be stated factually — no implication of superior ability, and there is no partial designation.

4. Recall scaffolding
A memorable compression: I protects the PROFESSION's face to the world; II protects MARKETS; III protects CLIENTS; IV protects EMPLOYERS; V protects the quality of the WORK; VI polices divided loyalties; VII protects the PROGRAM itself. Within III, the ordering A–E runs from the broadest duty (loyalty) to the narrowest mechanics (confidentiality). Item sets are built on the boundaries — what each subsection does NOT require — so the guidance readings that follow live at those edges.

Self-quiz
1. Who can trigger a Professional Conduct inquiry?
   -> Self-disclosure on the annual PCS, written complaints, media/public information, exam-administration reports, and CFA Institute monitoring.
2. Is a cautionary letter a public sanction?
   -> No — it is private; public censure, suspension, and revocation are the public sanctions.
3. A member rejects the staff-proposed sanction. What happens?
   -> The matter goes to a hearing panel of CFA Institute members; its decision is final within the program.
4. Client vs employer vs market integrity — rank the loyalties.
   -> Market/professional integrity first, clients second, employer third, self last.
5. Which Standard governs use of "CFA" as a noun?
   -> VII(B) — the designation is an adjective; "three CFAs" is improper use.
6. Under which subsection does plagiarism fall?
   -> I(C) Misrepresentation.
7. Written consent from all parties is the trigger phrase for which subsection?
   -> IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements.
8. What is the recommended record-retention period absent regulation, and whose property are work records?
   -> Seven years; records created in the scope of employment are the employer's property.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Guidance for Standard I: Professionalism

Orientation. Standard I is the widest net in the Code — five subsections covering law, independence, honesty, general conduct, and competence. Exam vignettes love I because nearly any fact pattern touches it; scoring depends on picking the RIGHT subsection and knowing the boundary cases (the strictest-law rule, the modest-gift line, the plagiarism carve-outs, and what Misconduct does not cover).

I(A) Knowledge of the Law. The operative rule is a maximum function: comply with the STRICTEST of (i) applicable law and regulation and (ii) the Code and Standards. "Applicable" can span several jurisdictions at once — where the member lives, where the firm operates, where clients reside — and the strictest applicable regime governs. Where law and the Code conflict, follow the stricter; where law requires something the Code forbids, follow the law and note the Code cannot compel illegality. Members are not required to be legal experts or to report violations to authorities (though reporting may be advisable or locally required); they ARE required to dissociate from illegal or unethical activity — first step: escalate to a supervisor or compliance; if the conduct continues, remove themselves from the matter, and resignation may ultimately be necessary. Remaining passively attached to a violation is itself a violation.

I(B) Independence and Objectivity. Do not offer, solicit, or accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or consideration that could reasonably be expected to compromise independence and objectivity — one's own or someone else's. The famous boundary: token, modest items are acceptable; lavish entertainment, paid travel on an issuer's aircraft, and allocations of hot IPOs to personal accounts are not. Gifts from CLIENTS (e.g., a bonus for past performance) are distinguishable from gifts from parties trying to influence future conduct — client gifts may be accepted but must be disclosed to the employer, and best practice gets employer permission BEFORE accepting performance-contingent client arrangements. Issuer-paid research is permissible only with flat-fee (not success-based) compensation and full disclosure. Analysts under pressure from investment banking, from covered companies, or from their own firm's positions must firewall, restrict personal trading, and if necessary decline coverage rather than shade a view.

I(C) Misrepresentation. No knowing misrepresentation in investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities. Three recurring species: performance guarantees (nothing risky may be presented as assured — only genuinely guaranteed instrument features may be described as guaranteed); credential inflation; and plagiarism — using others' work without attribution, in any medium. Carve-outs candidates forget: widely recognized factual material from recognized statistical and reporting services may be used without attribution, and a firm's internal research products may be used by colleagues — work belongs to the firm. Everything else — quotations, models, charts, even paraphrases — needs attribution. Omission counts: leaving out facts whose absence makes a statement misleading is misrepresentation, and model outputs presented without their key assumptions can qualify.

I(D) Misconduct. Covers dishonesty, fraud, deceit, and any conduct reflecting adversely on professional reputation, integrity, or COMPETENCE. The boundary the exam tests: it targets professional character, not private life or civil disagreements — personal bankruptcy from misfortune is not a violation; bankruptcy involving fraud is. Abusing the Code by filing frivolous complaints against others is itself misconduct.

I(E) Competence. The affirmative duty to maintain the knowledge and skill the professional role requires — through continuing education, staying current with evolving practice and regulation, and declining engagements beyond one's competence rather than improvising. It converts "keep learning" from a recommendation into an obligation and pairs with I(D): acting while impaired or unqualified reflects adversely on competence.

Recommended procedures worth citing in constructed response: maintain current compliance files on applicable law across jurisdictions; establish firm gift policies with value limits and reporting; restrict IPO allocations to personal accounts; institute review/attribution procedures for research; document sources.

Classic patterns. "Which law applies?" → the strictest applicable one currently in force (a stricter law not yet effective does not govern today). "Analyst accepts flight and resort stay from covered company" → I(B) violation; commercial travel and modest meals are the compliant substitute. "We guarantee 12%" → I(C) always. "Used a colleague's model without credit" → not a violation if within the same firm; using it after LEAVING the firm without permission is IV(A) territory.

Self-quiz
1. Local law is silent; the Code requires disclosure; a third country's law where clients reside is stricter still. Which governs?
   -> The strictest applicable regime — here the third country's law for those relationships.
2. First procedural step upon discovering a supervisor's ongoing violation?
   -> Dissociate by escalating to supervisor/compliance; if it continues, remove oneself from the activity.
3. A grateful client sends a significant bonus for last year's results. Permitted?
   -> Yes, but it must be disclosed to the employer; prospective performance arrangements need employer consent in writing (that consent requirement lives in IV(B)).
4. When may issuer-paid research comply?
   -> Flat, non-contingent compensation, full disclosure of the arrangement, and independent analysis.
5. Name the two attribution carve-outs under I(C).
   -> Widely recognized factual data from recognized reporting services, and internal use of the firm's own research.
6. A member is convicted of a bar fight unrelated to work. I(D)?
   -> Generally outside I(D) unless it reflects on professional integrity or competence — dishonesty and fraud are the core, not private conduct per se.
7. What does I(E) add beyond I(D)?
   -> An affirmative, ongoing duty to maintain professional competence — not merely to avoid disgraceful conduct.
8. Are members required to report others' law violations to regulators?
   -> The Code does not require it (dissociation is required); local law may, and reporting may be prudent.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Guidance for Standard II: Integrity of Capital Markets

Orientation. Two subsections, both about the market as a whole rather than any client: II(A) Material Nonpublic Information and II(B) Market Manipulation. The testable substance is definitional — what makes information material, what makes it nonpublic, how the mosaic defense works — plus the manipulation taxonomy and the intent requirement.

II(A) Material Nonpublic Information. Members who possess material nonpublic information that could affect the value of an investment must not act or CAUSE OTHERS to act on it. Material: a reasonable investor would want it before deciding, or disclosure would be expected to move the price — earnings surprises, M&A, major contract wins/losses, credit rating actions, significant management changes, imminent large orders. Ambiguity cuts against materiality: the less reliable and less specific the source, the less material. Nonpublic: not yet disseminated to the marketplace generally; disclosure to a select group (an analyst call restricted to institutional clients) does NOT make information public. Once released through broad channels and given time to be absorbed, it is public.

The mosaic theory is the sanctioned path: conclusions assembled from PUBLIC information plus NONMATERIAL nonpublic information may be acted on and communicated, even if the assembled conclusion would itself be material had a company insider whispered it. Documentation of the mosaic is the analyst's protection. The industry-expert nuance: consulting experts is legitimate; receiving material nonpublic detail from them (or paying them to breach duties) is not.

Firms with legitimate cross-functional information flows manage the risk structurally: firewalls between departments, restricted lists (stop issuing recommendations while in possession), watch lists, personal-trading pre-clearance, and — for unintentional selective disclosure by an issuer — the demand for prompt public dissemination. A blackout on all trading of the affected name is the blunt but safe response while in possession.

II(B) Market Manipulation. Practices intended to distort prices or artificially inflate trading volume with intent to mislead market participants. Two families: transaction-based — trades or trade patterns creating a false impression of activity or price (wash trades, painting the tape, pump-and-dump exits, securing a dominant position to squeeze a price); and information-based — spreading false or misleading information, or promoting a position and quietly selling into the enthusiasm one created. INTENT is the hinge: legitimate trading that moves prices is not manipulation; trading FOR the purpose of misleading is. The curriculum's boundary example: transacting to exploit known market rigidities for profit is not automatically manipulation — the misleading purpose is what condemns it. Note the interaction pattern common in vignettes: talking a book up in the media and then dumping the position is II(B); doing it with insider knowledge stacks II(A) on top.

Classic patterns. Board member relays deal talk to a fund; the fund buys → II(A) violated by tipper and trader alike. Analyst aggregates channel checks, public filings, and a supplier's nonmaterial shipping schedule into a sell call → mosaic, compliant. Firm trades a thin stock back and forth between accounts before quarter-end → II(B) transaction-based. "Everyone will know tomorrow anyway" → still nonpublic today.

Self-quiz
1. Define material and nonpublic in one line each.
   -> Material: a reasonable investor would consider it important / price-relevant. Nonpublic: not yet broadly disseminated to the market.
2. Does disclosure on a clients-only conference call make information public?
   -> No — selective dissemination is still nonpublic.
3. State the mosaic theory and its limit.
   -> Acting on conclusions from public plus NONMATERIAL nonpublic information is permitted; receiving material nonpublic pieces poisons the mosaic.
4. What single feature distinguishes manipulation from aggressive but legal trading?
   -> Intent to mislead market participants (distorting prices or volume for that purpose).
5. Give one transaction-based and one information-based manipulation example.
   -> Wash trades inflating volume; spreading a false rumor (or promoting a holding and selling into the hype).
6. What should a firm do when it comes into possession of MNPI through its banking arm?
   -> Firewall the information, place the issuer on a restricted/watch list, suspend recommendations and proprietary trading in the name, and pre-clear personal trades.
7. An analyst pays an expert-network consultant who reveals unreleased quarterly numbers. Violation?
   -> Yes — II(A); using experts is fine, receiving (or inducing) material nonpublic disclosures is not.
8. Fund sells after its managers give glowing TV interviews and employees follow. Which standards?
   -> II(B) market manipulation (and the trading order engages VI(B); the interviews' honesty engages I(C)/V).

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Guidance for Standard III: Duties to Clients

Orientation. Five subsections, and the densest source of item-set questions in the topic: III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care; III(B) Fair Dealing; III(C) Suitability; III(D) Performance Presentation; III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality. The recurring skill is identifying WHO the client is, WHAT the mandate is, and WHICH subsection the fact pattern actually engages.

III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care. Client interests come before the employer's and one's own. Prudence means the care, skill, and judgment of a person of like capacity in a like role; loyalty means managing for the client's benefit, not the manager's. The identity question is the classic trap: for a pension plan, the client is the plan BENEFICIARIES — not the sponsor who hired the manager — so following sponsor instructions that harm beneficiaries violates III(A). Soft dollars are analyzed here: client brokerage belongs to the client, so it may be used only to benefit the client — research that aids the investment decision-making process qualifies; directed-brokerage requests from the client are honored with best execution and disclosure that the client may not be getting the full benefit.

III(B) Fair Dealing. Fair and objective treatment of ALL clients when providing recommendations or taking investment action. Fair ≠ identical: premium service levels may be sold openly, but no client may be systematically disadvantaged on material investment information or allocations. Mechanics that appear on exams: disseminate recommendation changes so all affected clients have a fair shot (simultaneous or tiered-but-fair release; brief window before executing discretionary trades); allocate block trades and IPOs pro rata on suitability, never by account size or favoritism; do not front-run one client set for another; and oversubscribed hot issues should not be allocated to the accounts of the firm's principals at all.

III(C) Suitability. Two regimes. Advisory relationship: make reasonable inquiry into experience, objectives, constraints, and risk/return profile BEFORE acting; document an IPS; update it regularly (annually is the cited practice); and judge every recommendation in the context of the client's TOTAL portfolio — a risky position can be suitable inside a diversified whole, and a client's other holdings can rescue or condemn a strategy. Unsolicited trade requests that are unsuitable: advise the client, seek to have the account reclassified or documented as client-directed, or decline. Mandate/index management: suitability means fidelity to the stated mandate — the manager owes the STRATEGY to whoever buys it and need not assess each fund investor's profile.

III(D) Performance Presentation. Performance communication must be fair, accurate, and complete: no cherry-picked accounts or periods, no claiming another firm's or team's record as one's own without disclosure (portability requires the record to remain the achieving firm's), composites should include ALL similar portfolios — including terminated accounts for the periods they existed — and simulated or model results must be labeled as such. GIPS compliance is the recommended, not required, path; a firm may present honestly without GIPS but may not claim compliance falsely.

III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality. Information about current, FORMER, and prospective clients stays confidential, with three gates: the information concerns illegal activities; law requires disclosure; or the client permits it. Cooperation with a CFA Institute PCP investigation is treated as compatible with the standard. Status itself is confidential — confirming that someone IS a client can be a breach. Electronic-era corollary: guard client data in transmission and storage; disclosing to third-party vendors requires care and, ideally, consent.

Classic patterns. "Manager tilts plan assets to please the sponsoring company" → III(A), client = beneficiaries. "Top-tier clients get the upgrade call an hour early by design, others after execution" → III(B). "Aggressive strategy for a 60-year-old facing withdrawals" → III(C) unless the total-portfolio context changes the answer. "Composite drops terminated accounts" → III(D). "Tells a friend which celebrities the firm manages" → III(E).

Self-quiz
1. For a corporate pension mandate, who is the client under III(A)?
   -> The plan beneficiaries — not the sponsoring company.
2. What two conditions legitimize soft-dollar use of client brokerage?
   -> Best execution and use of the commissions to benefit the client (research aiding investment decisions).
3. Does fair dealing require identical treatment?
   -> No — different openly offered service levels are fine; systematic disadvantage on material information or allocations is not.
4. How must oversubscribed IPOs be allocated?
   -> Pro rata across suitable client accounts; firm principals' accounts should be excluded from hot issues.
5. State the total-portfolio principle.
   -> Suitability is judged against the client's entire portfolio and circumstances — a position risky alone may be suitable within the whole (and vice versa).
6. Manager runs an index fund; must she assess each investor's risk profile?
   -> No — in mandate management, suitability means managing faithfully to the stated mandate.
7. Three exceptions to confidentiality?
   -> Illegal activity, legally required disclosure, client permission.
8. May terminated accounts be dropped from a composite's history?
   -> No — they remain for the periods they existed; excluding them biases the record (survivorship).
9. Is GIPS compliance required by III(D)?
   -> No — recommended; the requirement is fair, accurate, complete presentation, and no false compliance claims.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Guidance for Standard IV: Duties to Employers

Orientation. Three subsections — IV(A) Loyalty, IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements, IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors. The examinable core is the departing-employee doctrine, the written-consent trigger, and what "reasonable supervision" demands when a compliance system is weak.

IV(A) Loyalty. While employed, act for the employer's benefit and do not deprive the employer of your skills' advantage, divulge confidential information, or otherwise cause harm. Independent practice that COMPETES with the employer requires notification and the employer's CONSENT before it begins — describing the services, expected duration, and compensation. Preparing to leave is permitted (interviewing, arranging financing); actively competing before departure is not. On departure, the dividing line is property vs. skill: general knowledge, experience, and skills leave with the employee; client lists, records, models, marketing materials, and anything built on the employer's time or systems stay — taking them (even by memory-dumping a client list reconstructed from firm records) violates IV(A). Contacting former clients AFTER departure using public information is permissible absent a binding non-compete. Social media blurs the line: accounts and content created for the employer's business are employer property. Whistleblowing sits as the exception: acts contrary to the employer's interest are defensible only when protecting clients or market integrity — never for personal gain.

IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements. No gifts, benefits, compensation, or consideration that competes with, or might reasonably create a conflict with, the employer's interest — UNLESS written consent is obtained from ALL PARTIES involved (employer and the outside payer; email counts as written). The archetype is the client-paid performance bonus or the directorship stipend: acceptable only after written disclosure of terms (nature, amount, duration) and written employer consent. Contrast with I(B): I(B) polices influence on independence from any source; IV(B) polices side-compensation that could divide loyalty to the employer, and its cure is specifically written consent in advance.

IV(C) Responsibilities of Supervisors. Anyone with supervisory authority must make REASONABLE efforts to detect and prevent violations of laws, regulations, and the Code by those under their authority — delegation spreads the work but not the responsibility. The standard is effort and system quality, not omniscience: a violation under a robust, well-enforced system is not automatically the supervisor's violation. The two testable action rules: (1) if the firm's compliance system is inadequate, the member should DECLINE supervisory responsibility in writing until the firm fixes it — accepting authority over an unenforceable system is itself the breach; (2) upon discovering a violation, it is NOT enough to warn the wrongdoer and resume normal operations — the supervisor must respond promptly, investigate, and limit the wrongdoer's activity (increase supervision, suspend the activity) until the investigation concludes. An effective program has clear, understandable written procedures, designated compliance ownership, permitted-conduct guidance, escalation routes, and once a violation surfaces, visible enforcement.

Classic patterns. "Analyst emails himself the valuation model before resigning" → IV(A). "Manages a friend's portfolio on weekends for a fee, employer unaware" → IV(A) (competing practice without consent) and IV(B) once compensated. "Client offers a car if the portfolio beats 15%" → IV(B): written consent from employer required before accepting. "Supervisor learns a trader front-ran and simply tells him to stop" → IV(C) inadequate response. "Offered a compliance-officer role at a firm with no real procedures" → decline in writing until procedures are adopted.

Self-quiz
1. What may a departing employee take, and what must stay?
   -> General skill, knowledge, and experience leave; client lists, records, models, and materials created for the employer stay.
2. May a member solicit her former clients after resigning?
   -> Yes, using public information and absent an enforceable non-compete — solicitation BEFORE departure violates IV(A).
3. What does independent competing practice require before it begins?
   -> Notification describing services, duration, and compensation — and the employer's consent.
4. State IV(B)'s cure precisely.
   -> Written consent from all parties involved (employer and outside compensator), obtained in advance, after written disclosure of the arrangement's terms.
5. Distinguish I(B) from IV(B) in one sentence.
   -> I(B) protects independence and objectivity from outside influence; IV(B) protects the employer from divided loyalty created by side compensation, cured by written consent.
6. When must a member refuse supervisory duties?
   -> When the compliance system is inadequate to let supervision be exercised reasonably — decline in writing until it is fixed.
7. A violation occurs despite an excellent, enforced system. Did the supervisor violate IV(C)?
   -> Not necessarily — the standard is reasonable efforts and adequate systems, not strict liability.
8. What is the minimum adequate response to a discovered violation?
   -> Prompt investigation plus restricting the wrongdoer's activity pending its outcome — a warning alone is insufficient.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Guidance for Standard V: Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions

Orientation. Three subsections that police the QUALITY of the work product: V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis, V(B) Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients, V(C) Record Retention. The exam concentrates on third-party research due diligence, the fact-versus-opinion line, and what changes in process must be proactively disclosed.

V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis. Every recommendation and investment action needs thoroughness and diligence proportional to the claim being made, and a reasonable, adequate basis supported by research. What counts as reasonable scales with context: the firm's stated methodology, the product's risk, the client base. The recurring applications:
- Third-party research and vendors: reliance is permitted only after a reasonable and diligent effort to verify soundness — assess the assumptions, rigor, timeliness, analyst objectivity, and the vendor's competence, periodically re-checked. "A former colleague built their research department" is not due diligence. A stale update cadence for a fast-moving, high-risk sector fails the timeliness leg even if the original work was rigorous.
- Quantitative models and screens (including externally developed ones and AI-assisted tools): understand the assumptions and limitations well enough to judge output; test scenarios beyond the fitted sample; a member need not be the model's author but cannot treat it as a black box.
- Group research: a member who disagrees with a consensus view need not dissociate from the report IF the conclusion has a reasonable and adequate basis reached through a sound process — the name can stay on it; documenting the difference of opinion is prudent.
- Fund and manager selection: the same diligence duty extends to selecting sub-advisers and pooled vehicles — review process, personnel, terms, and adherence to stated strategy.

V(B) Communication with Clients and Prospective Clients. Four obligations, all frequently quoted verbatim in stems: (1) disclose the basic FORMAT and general principles of the investment process, and disclose promptly any CHANGES that materially affect it — a shift in strategy, a new risk model, a change in the team running the mandate; (2) disclose significant LIMITATIONS and RISKS associated with the process — liquidity constraints, leverage, capacity limits, the assumptions a projection rests on; (3) identify important factors behind recommendations and include them or offer them; (4) distinguish FACT from OPINION. The last is the most-tested line: valuations, forecasts, and expected returns are opinions and must read as such; "the fund WILL return X" states an opinion as fact and simultaneously trips I(C)'s guarantee prohibition. Balanced communication also means the risks travel with the pitch — an upside scenario without its assumptions and downside is a violation even if every number is honestly derived. Communication is medium-neutral: calls, emails, social posts, and slide decks all count.

V(C) Record Retention. Develop and maintain the records that SUPPORT the analysis, recommendations, actions, and communications — the paper trail that lets the reasonable basis be reconstructed. Records created in the course of employment are the FIRM's property: a departing analyst may not take supporting research files, and rebuilding a recommendation history at a new firm requires regenerating the supporting work from public/new sources, not memory-reconstructing the old firm's files. Retention obligations are set by regulation where regulation speaks; where it is silent, seven years is the recommended benchmark. Personal notes and working models used in forming recommendations are within the retention perimeter — a policy that keeps final reports but discards the supporting notes and models fails the standard.

Classic patterns. "Fund relies on a broker's research; the broker is owned by the fund's founders and updates annually in a volatile sector" → V(A) diligence failure (and VI conflicts). "Committee report; one member's model disagrees" → stay on the report if the process was sound. "Marketing piece with a regression-based projection, risks disclosed" → compliant opinion; the same claim phrased as a certainty → V(B) + I(C). "Firm keeps final PDFs, deletes analyst notebooks after a year" → V(C).

Self-quiz
1. What must be verified before relying on third-party research?
   -> Soundness of assumptions, rigor and timeliness of analysis, objectivity and competence of the source — with periodic re-review.
2. May a member's name remain on a group report she disagrees with?
   -> Yes, if the consensus conclusion has a reasonable and adequate basis from a sound process.
3. What triggers a proactive client communication under V(B)?
   -> Any material change to the investment process, strategy, or the people/constraints that shape it.
4. State the fact-vs-opinion rule and its classic violation phrasing.
   -> Forecasts and valuations must be presented as opinion; "we WILL earn X%" presents opinion as fact (and edges into a guarantee under I(C)).
5. Whose property are the research records an analyst creates at work?
   -> The employer's — they stay behind at departure.
6. Recommended retention period absent regulation?
   -> Seven years.
7. Does V(A) apply to picking an external manager or fund?
   -> Yes — the same diligence and reasonable-basis duty covers selection of sub-advisers, funds, and vendors.
8. An analyst uses a screening model he cannot explain. Compliant?
   -> No — he must understand the model's assumptions and limitations well enough to judge its output; black-box reliance fails V(A).

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Guidance for Standard VI: Conflicts of Interest

Orientation. Three subsections about divided incentives: VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts (titled Disclosure of Conflicts in older materials), VI(B) Priority of Transactions, VI(C) Referral Fees. The testable machinery is what must be disclosed and to whom, the client–employer–self trade ordering, and the before-the-fact timing of referral disclosure.

VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts. The updated standard states a preference ordering: AVOID conflicts where possible; where a conflict cannot reasonably be avoided, make full and fair disclosure — to clients and prospects for matters that could reasonably be expected to impair independence/objectivity or interfere with duties to them, and to the EMPLOYER for matters that interfere with duties to it. Disclosure must be prominent, in plain language, and communicate the relevant information effectively — burying it in a footnote fails. The standing inventory of disclosable conflicts: ownership of covered securities by the analyst or firm, directorships and outside board service, underwriting and other investment-banking relationships with covered issuers, market-making in covered names, referral and fee arrangements, and compensation structures that could shape behavior — a performance-fee share that rewards short-term risk-taking is disclosable to clients even if the firm forbids discussing compensation, and if firm policy makes the required disclosure impossible, the compliant paths are changing the arrangement or the policy, not silence.

VI(B) Priority of Transactions. Investment transactions for CLIENTS and EMPLOYERS take precedence over transactions in which a member or candidate is a beneficial owner. Beneficial ownership reaches accounts the member influences or from which the member benefits — personal, spouse, family, trusts. The ordering rule, not a trading ban: personal trading is permitted once clients and the employer have had adequate opportunity to act. Family accounts that are ordinary fee-paying CLIENT accounts must be treated like any client — disadvantaging them is itself a violation. Standard machinery: pre-clearance, restricted periods around recommendations, holding-period rules, IPO/private-placement restrictions for access persons, duplicate confirmations to compliance. The classic passing pattern: the firm (clients) trades first, employees after — the ORDER complies with VI(B) even when the surrounding conduct violates other standards.

VI(C) Referral Fees. Disclose to the employer, and to clients and prospects, any compensation, consideration, or benefit received FROM or paid TO others for the recommendation of products or services — BEFORE entering into any formal agreement, so the client can weigh the recommendation's partiality and its full cost. Disclosure covers the nature and value of the consideration: cash, fee splits, reciprocal service arrangements, soft benefits. Inter-departmental referral bonuses inside one firm count. The timing is the tested detail: disclosure after the engagement is a violation even if terms were otherwise fair.

Classic patterns. "Analyst covers a company on whose board she sits" → VI(A) disclose (and manage MNPI exposure under II(A)). "Founders own the broker that underwrote the covered stock" → VI(A) full disclosure of ownership + underwriting + reliance. "Manager buys for his own account before releasing the upgrade" → VI(B) front-running. "Accountant sends clients; adviser sends a percentage back; clients never told" → VI(C). "Compensation package tied to quarterly performance fees, non-disclosable by firm policy" → VI(A): renegotiate the package or the policy.

Self-quiz
1. State VI(A)'s ordering of remedies.
   -> Avoid the conflict where possible; otherwise full, fair, prominent, plain-language disclosure to those affected (clients/prospects; employer for employer-facing conflicts).
2. Name four standing conflicts on the disclosure inventory.
   -> Security ownership, board/directorship service, investment-banking/underwriting relationships, market-making; also referral arrangements and incentive-fee structures.
3. Who ranks ahead of whom under VI(B)?
   -> Client and employer transactions before any account in which the member has beneficial ownership.
4. Are family client accounts subordinated to other clients?
   -> No — fee-paying family accounts are clients and must not be disadvantaged (nor favored).
5. When must a referral arrangement be disclosed?
   -> Before entering the formal referral agreement / before the client engages — with the nature and value of the consideration.
6. A firm's clients sold first; employees sold immediately after. VI(B)?
   -> The order of trading complies with VI(B) — whatever other standards the episode violates.
7. Firm policy forbids revealing compensation, but the member's incentive fee is a client-facing conflict. What now?
   -> Silence is not compliant — seek to change the arrangement or the policy so required disclosure can be made.
8. Is avoiding all personal trading required?
   -> No — VI(B) sets priority, not prohibition; trade after clients and employer have had adequate opportunity, within firm pre-clearance rules.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Guidance for Standard VII: Responsibilities as a CFA Institute Member or Candidate

Orientation. Two subsections guarding the program itself: VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs, VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the CFA Program. Nearly every exam question here is a wording question — what may be said about candidacy, passing, and the designation, and what crosses into exaggeration.

VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs. No conduct that compromises the reputation or INTEGRITY of CFA Institute or the integrity, validity, or SECURITY of its programs. The enumerated sins: cheating on any CFA Institute exam (or assisting others); disclosing confidential exam content — questions seen, topics tested, formulas asked — in any medium including forums and study groups, before or after exam day; misusing volunteer positions (graders, item writers, society officers) or the information they confer; misrepresenting information on the Professional Conduct Statement; and improperly using association with CFA Institute for personal or professional gain. The boundary the exam checks: expressing OPINIONS about the program — criticizing the curriculum, the exam's difficulty, or CFA Institute policy — is fully permitted; revealing CONTENT is not. Candidates comparing broad topic weightings already published by CFA Institute are fine; "question 3 was on Singer–Terhaar" is not.

VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the Designation, and the Program. The governing principle: no exaggerating the MEANING or IMPLICATIONS of membership, candidacy, or the designation. The mechanical rules that decide most questions:
- The designation is an ADJECTIVE, never a noun: "Jane Smith, CFA" and "a CFA charterholder" comply; "she is a CFA" and "three CFAs on staff" do not.
- Candidacy may be claimed only while REGISTERED for the next scheduled exam; between registrations one is not a candidate. There is no such thing as "CFA Level II" as a title; "passed Level II of the CFA Program" is the compliant phrasing.
- Passing all levels on the first attempt may be stated as FACT; wrapping it in claims of superior ability crosses the line.
- No partial designation, no "CFA (expected 2027)", no implying the charter guarantees performance — statements that membership or the charter means superior results violate the standard even in marketing superlatives ("CFA Institute recognizes our expertise, so you can rely on superior returns").
- Membership claims ("member of CFA Institute") require the membership actually be active — dues paid and PCS filed; letting membership lapse suspends the right to use the designation.
- Truthful, factual descriptions of the program's rigor or the requirements to earn the charter are always permitted.

Classic patterns. "Our team are all CFA's" → noun misuse. "CFA candidate" on a résumé by someone not registered for the next window → violation; listing exams passed factually → compliant. Telling a colleague the afternoon session "leaned heavily on a specific ethics case" → VII(A). A society volunteer trading on knowledge from a graders' meeting → VII(A) misuse of position.

Self-quiz
1. What may a candidate say publicly about the exam just taken?
   -> Opinions about difficulty, quality, or the program — but nothing about the content: no questions, tested topics, or formulas.
2. When is "I am a CFA candidate" accurate?
   -> Only while registered for the next scheduled exam.
3. Correct the phrase: "We employ four CFAs."
   -> "We employ four CFA charterholders" — the designation is an adjective, not a noun.
4. May a member state she passed all three levels consecutively on first attempts?
   -> Yes, as a statement of fact — provided no claim of superior ability is attached.
5. What keeps the right to use the designation alive?
   -> Active membership: dues current and the annual Professional Conduct Statement filed.
6. A firm's ad implies CFA Institute endorses its managers' performance. Which standard?
   -> VII(B) — exaggerating the implications of the designation/membership.
7. Does criticizing CFA Institute policy violate VII(A)?
   -> No — opinions about the organization and program are expressly permitted.
8. A grader hints to a friend which case types to drill. Violation?
   -> Yes — VII(A): misuse of a volunteer position and disclosure of confidential program information.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
Reading: Application of the Code and Standards: Level III

Orientation. This reading adds no new rules — it is the case workbook that exercises every guidance reading at exam realism: multi-issue vignettes where several standards fire at once and the task is to name WHICH standard, WHO violated it, and WHAT the compliant action would have been. The skill being graded is issue-spotting plus precision of citation, so this note is a method note: how to work an ethics case, the standard co-occurrence clusters, and the discriminations that separate near-miss answers.

1. Working method for ethics vignettes
Read once for the cast and their roles (who advises whom; who supervises whom; who is a member/candidate). Read again flagging ACTIONS — each verb is a potential violation: accepted, disclosed, traded, told, omitted, delegated, signed. For each flagged action ask three questions in order: (i) which relationship does it strain — market, client, employer, program? (that selects the Standard number); (ii) which subsection's specific duty does it engage? (iii) is there a curing fact in the vignette — written consent, disclosure, pre-clearance, a sound process — that makes the conduct compliant? Most wrong answers come from stopping at (ii) and missing the cure, or from citing a neighboring subsection (I(B) for IV(B), III(B) for VI(B)).

2. The recurring co-occurrence clusters
- MNPI cluster: an insider tip → II(A) for tipper and trader; trading the name afterward engages VI(B) ordering; media promotion around the trade adds II(B); firm response is firewalls/restricted lists.
- Performance-marketing cluster: guaranteed or fact-stated projections → V(B) + I(C); cherry-picked composites or dropped terminated accounts → III(D); designation puffery in the same brochure → VII(B).
- Departing-employee cluster: pre-departure solicitation or taken materials → IV(A); post-departure contact from public sources → compliant; reconstructed research files → V(C) + IV(A).
- Side-money cluster: client bonuses and directorships → IV(B) written consent; the same facts viewed as influence on analysis → I(B); undisclosed referral splits → VI(C).
- Supervision cluster: weak systems → decline in writing (IV(C)); discovered violation → investigate and restrict, never warn-and-resume; the underlying act keeps its own citation.
- Suitability cluster: aggressive strategies near liquidity events → III(C) total-portfolio test; mandate funds → suitability = fidelity to mandate; IPS review cadence.

3. High-yield discriminations
I(B) vs IV(B): influence on objectivity vs divided loyalty to the employer — the cure for IV(B) is written consent of all parties. II(A) vs V(A): acting on a material tip vs acting without adequate research — a rigorous mosaic cures the first concern, documented diligence the second. III(B) vs VI(B): unfair treatment across clients vs putting personal trades ahead of clients. III(D) vs I(C): performance record presentation vs any other misstatement — performance claims cite III(D) first when a track record is the subject. VII(B) vs I(C): credential puffery cites VII(B); non-credential misstatements cite I(C). IV(C) attaches only to people with supervisory AUTHORITY — peers who observe misconduct dissociate under I(A), they do not "violate IV(C)."

4. Constructed-response habits
Cite standard number AND name ("Standard III(C) — Suitability"); state the violating fact in one clause; state the compliant alternative in one clause. When a question asks for the standard LEAST likely violated, verify each option against the vignette's curing facts — least-likely questions are usually decided by one action that was actually done correctly (the trade ordering, a disclosure that did occur). When asked what someone SHOULD do, prefer the remediation ladder the guidance readings teach: disclose → obtain consent → restrict/dissociate → escalate → refuse/resign, choosing the least drastic step that cures the specific breach.

Self-quiz
1. Name the three questions to ask of every flagged action in a vignette.
   -> Which relationship does it strain (Standard); which subsection's duty does it engage; is there a curing fact making it compliant?
2. A fund trades on a director's tip, promotes the stock on TV, then sells; employees sell after the fund. List the citations.
   -> II(A) MNPI (tip and trade), II(B) market manipulation (promotion then sale); the fund-before-employees ORDER itself complies with VI(B).
3. Which standard pair is separated by the phrase "written consent from all parties"?
   -> IV(B) Additional Compensation (the consent cure) vs I(B) Independence and Objectivity (no consent cure — influence must be avoided/managed).
4. When does IV(C) apply to a person at all?
   -> Only when they hold supervisory authority; observers without authority dissociate under I(A) instead.
5. Performance projection stated as certainty in a brochure — first two citations?
   -> V(B) fact/opinion (and balanced disclosure) plus I(C) misrepresentation/guarantee.
6. What decides most "least likely violated" questions?
   -> The one action performed correctly — a cure present in the vignette (proper ordering, actual disclosure, documented diligence).
7. Give the remediation ladder in order.
   -> Disclose → obtain consent → restrict or dissociate → escalate to supervisor/compliance → refuse the role or resign.
8. In grading terms, what earns the point on a violation question?
   -> Naming the standard by number and name, tying it to the specific fact, and stating the compliant alternative.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Asset Allocation
Reading: Asset Allocation with Real-World Constraints
Orientation. Only five LOS, but the tax LOS is the one that carries real math — after-tax return, after-tax risk, and the widened rebalancing band. The rest is applied judgment: how asset size, liquidity, horizon, and regulation constrain the allocation; when to revise the strategic allocation versus make a temporary tactical shift; and the behavioral biases that derail allocation, with their remedies. Learn the tax formulas cold and know the bias-remedy pairs.

LOS 1 — Asset size, liquidity, horizon, and regulatory constraints
LOS: discuss asset size, liquidity needs, time horizon, and regulatory or other considerations as constraints on asset allocation
What you must do:  Explain how each constraint limits the feasible allocation, especially the two-sided effect of asset size.
Asset size
Size cuts both ways. A very large fund faces capacity limits and market impact — it can't move the needle with small or niche asset classes and is pushed toward large-cap, liquid holdings — though it CAN access scale-only strategies (large private equity, direct real estate). A very small fund is blocked from asset classes with high minimums (private equity, hedge funds, direct real estate), can't diversify across enough managers, and may lack the resources for alternatives due diligence.
Table 1 — Asset size as a constraint
Size
Core challenge
Consequence for allocation
Very large
Capacity limits and market impact
Pushed toward large-cap/liquid; niche classes can't move the needle; but can access scale-only alternatives
Very small
High minimums, thin resources
Blocked from PE / hedge funds / direct real estate; can't diversify across managers; limited due-diligence capacity
Liquidity, time horizon, and regulation
	•	Liquidity — the investor's cash needs (spending, redemptions, capital calls) cap the allocation to illiquid assets; weigh both the investor's needs AND the asset classes' liquidity, including under stress. Low-liquidity-need investors (large endowments, sovereign funds) can hold more illiquids.
	•	Time horizon — a longer horizon supports more equity, illiquidity, and risk; horizon shortens over the life cycle as human capital declines, and many investors have multi-stage horizons (e.g., pre- and post-retirement).
	•	Regulatory and other — pensions face funding rules; insurers face risk-based capital and asset restrictions and must match liabilities; banks face regulatory capital and liquidity coverage; foundations face spending rules (e.g., the US 5% payout); sovereign funds face political and legal constraints.
Liquidity budgeting: beyond a single liquidity figure, assess time-to-liquidate for each holding and model liquidity under stress (correlations and redemption demands spike together). Committed but undrawn capital calls to private funds are a future liquidity claim that must be reserved for.
Table 1b — Dominant constraints by institution type
Institution
Dominant constraints
DB pension
Funding rules; liability matching; the sponsor's covenant/ability to contribute
Insurer
Risk-based capital; asset-eligibility rules; duration matching of liabilities
Bank
Regulatory capital; liquidity coverage; asset–liability management
Endowment / foundation
Spending rule (often ~5%); long horizon; UPMIFA prudence standard
Sovereign wealth fund
Political and legal constraints; transparency; sometimes a very long horizon
Exam focus: The favorite point is that asset size constrains BOTH ways — small funds are locked out of alternatives, large funds are constrained by capacity and market impact. For institutions, tie the constraint to the investor type (insurer → capital rules + liability matching; foundation → spending rule).
LOS 2 — Tax considerations in allocation and rebalancing
LOS: discuss tax considerations in asset allocation and rebalancing
What you must do:  Compute after-tax return and risk, widen the rebalancing band for taxes, and apply asset location.
Taxes reduce both return and risk, because the government effectively shares in gains and losses. The core relationships:
After-tax return:   r_at = r_pt × (1 − t)
Decomposed:         r_at = p_i·r·(1 − t_i) + p_cg·r·(1 − t_cg)
After-tax risk:     σ_at = σ_pt × (1 − t)
After-tax range:    R_at = R_pt / (1 − t)     (WIDER when taxed)
Worked example — after-tax return (income vs gains)
A portfolio's 7% pre-tax return is 30% income (taxed at 35%) and 70% price appreciation (taxed at 20%).
r_at = 0.30 × 7 × (1 − 0.35) + 0.70 × 7 × (1 − 0.20)
     = 0.30 × 7 × 0.65   +   0.70 × 7 × 0.80
     = 1.365            +   3.92
     = 5.285%   ≈  5.29%
Worked example — after-tax risk and the rebalancing band
Pre-tax volatility σ_pt = 15%; blended tax rate t = 25%; pre-tax rebalancing corridor ±10%.
σ_at = 15% × (1 − 0.25)   = 11.25%   (lower after-tax risk)
R_at = 10% / (1 − 0.25)   = 13.33%   (wider band)
Because after-tax volatility is lower, the same economic risk tolerance corresponds to a WIDER rebalancing corridor for the taxable investor.
Asset location and tax management
Asset location (distinct from asset allocation) places tax-inefficient assets — taxable bonds, high-turnover strategies, REITs — in tax-deferred or tax-exempt accounts, and tax-efficient assets — low-turnover equities held for long-term gains — in taxable accounts. Add tax-loss harvesting, holding for long-term rates, low turnover, and municipal bonds for high-bracket investors. Taxable investors also rebalance LESS often and use cash flows to limit realized gains.
Tax-efficient techniques worth naming: harvest losses to offset realized gains; hold winners for long-term rates and let unrealized gains compound (the deferral itself is valuable, since untaxed money keeps earning); donate appreciated securities or pass them on at death, where a basis step-up can erase the embedded gain; and rebalance using new contributions or by directing income distributions rather than by selling appreciated positions.
Exam focus: High-probability calculations: after-tax return (both the blended and the income/gains-decomposed forms), after-tax volatility, and the widened corridor R/(1−t). Qualitatively, know asset location and that taxable investors rebalance less frequently.
LOS 3 — Revising the strategic asset allocation
LOS: recommend and justify revisions to an asset allocation given change(s) in investment objectives and/or constraints
What you must do:  Identify what triggers a strategic revision and recommend the change, distinguishing it from a tactical shift.
Revisit the strategic asset allocation (SAA) when the inputs that produced it change materially:
	•	Objectives — a changed return requirement or new goals (e.g., a new spending need).
	•	Constraints — a shortening horizon, rising liquidity needs, a change in tax status, new regulation, or a large change in wealth.
	•	Beliefs — a significant, durable change in capital market expectations.
A strategic revision permanently changes the SAA and its target weights; it is different from a tactical shift, which is a temporary deviation to exploit a short-term opportunity (LOS 4). Life events — retirement, an inheritance, a liquidity event, a new regulation — are the classic revision triggers.
Drill: A client retires and shifts from saving to drawing income. What SAA revision follows? (Answer: shorten effective horizon and de-risk — more fixed income and liquidity to fund withdrawals and manage sequence-of-returns risk, since human capital is now largely spent.)
Exam focus: Constructed-response: given a described life or regulatory change, recommend a specific SAA revision and justify via the changed objective or constraint. Be explicit that this is a STRATEGIC change, not a tactical bet.
LOS 4 — Short-term (tactical) shifts in asset allocation
LOS: discuss the use of short-term shifts in asset allocation
What you must do:  Explain what tactical asset allocation is, its two forms, the signals it uses, and its costs.
Tactical asset allocation (TAA) is a deliberate short-term deviation from the SAA to exploit perceived near-term opportunities (mispricing, cyclical signals). It is always measured AGAINST the SAA: success means adding return or reducing risk relative to the strategic mix.
Table 2 — Two forms of TAA
Form
Basis
Typical signals
Discretionary TAA
Manager judgment and forecasts
Business cycle, monetary policy, relative valuation
Systematic TAA
Rules-based, repeatable signals
Value, momentum, carry, trend-following
Signals in practice: economic (cycle, policy), valuation (earnings yield vs bond yield, credit spreads), sentiment (VIX, put/call ratios, margin debt), and momentum/trend. 
Costs and evaluation: TAA incurs transaction costs and taxes and adds active risk, so it must overcome those hurdles to add value. Evaluate it by the improvement in Sharpe ratio versus the SAA, the information ratio, and the hit rate (percentage of correct calls).
A note on valuation signals and realism: a common relative-value signal is the gap between the equity earnings yield and the government bond yield (the "Fed model" idea) — a wide gap argues for equities. But most evidence shows TAA is hard to profit from consistently once transaction costs, taxes, and wrong calls are netted out, so it should be sized modestly against the SAA.
Exam focus: Distinguish TAA (temporary, exploits short-term opportunity) from a strategic revision (permanent, driven by changed objectives/constraints). Know the discretionary vs systematic split and that TAA is judged relative to the SAA net of costs.
LOS 5 — Behavioral biases in asset allocation
LOS: identify behavioral biases that arise in asset allocation and recommend methods to overcome them
What you must do:  Identify a bias from a described behavior and prescribe the remedy.
Table 3 — Biases in allocation and how to counter them
Bias
Effect on allocation
Remedy
Loss aversion
Holds losers, avoids risk it should take
Goals-based framing; a disciplined written policy
Illusion of control
Over-trading, concentrated bets
Anchor to the global market portfolio; diversify; evidence-based process
Mental accounting
Sub-optimal aggregate portfolio
Use goals-based framing constructively; also review total risk
Representativeness / recency
Performance chasing
Disciplined rebalancing; long-term capital market expectations
Availability / framing
Overweights vivid recent events
Base rates; present choices in multiple frames; formal review
The general antidotes run through every bias: a formal investment policy statement, a disciplined rebalancing policy, using the global market portfolio as the neutral anchor, and goals-based framing that aligns the portfolio with how clients actually think about money.
Three more that surface in allocation: overconfidence (over-trading, under-diversification, and excessive active risk — countered by an honest track-record review and a systematic process); home bias (overweighting domestic assets relative to the global market portfolio — countered by anchoring to the GMP and justifying any deviation); and regret aversion (herding or inaction to avoid the pain of a future wrong decision — countered by a pre-committed policy and rebalancing rules).
Exam focus: Vignettes describe a behavior and ask for the bias and its cure. High-frequency pairs: performance chasing → recency/representativeness (fix: rebalancing + long-term CME); over-trading and concentration → illusion of control / overconfidence (fix: GMP anchor, systematic process); refusing to sell a loser → loss aversion (fix: disciplined policy).
Quick reference — key formulas
The tax formulas worth memorizing
After-tax return:  r_at = r_pt × (1 − t)
  decomposed:      r_at = p_i·r·(1 − t_i) + p_cg·r·(1 − t_cg)
After-tax risk:    σ_at = σ_pt × (1 − t)
After-tax range:   R_at = R_pt / (1 − t)     (wider)
Asset location: tax-inefficient assets -> tax-deferred/exempt;
                tax-efficient assets    -> taxable
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Name a constraint that binds a VERY LARGE fund and one that binds a VERY SMALL fund.
   -> Large: capacity limits / market impact. Small: high minimums block PE/HF/direct real estate.
2. Write the after-tax volatility formula.
   -> σ_at = σ_pt × (1 − t).
3. Should a taxable portfolio use wider or narrower rebalancing bands, and by what factor?
   -> Wider — the range scales up by 1/(1 − t).
4. Under asset location, where do tax-inefficient bonds and REITs go?
   -> In tax-deferred/exempt accounts; keep tax-efficient equities in taxable accounts.
5. Strategic revision vs tactical shift — what's the distinction?
   -> Strategic = permanent change to the SAA from changed objectives/constraints/beliefs; tactical = temporary bet on a short-term opportunity.
6. Name the two forms of TAA.
   -> Discretionary (judgment) and systematic (rules-based signals).
7. A client keeps chasing last year's best performers. Bias and remedy?
   -> Representativeness/recency; disciplined rebalancing and long-term CME.
8. What single anchor best counters the illusion of control?
   -> The global market portfolio as the neutral baseline.


Deep Dive A — After-tax mechanics, worked end to end
Taxes change three numbers the allocator uses: expected return, volatility, and the rebalancing math. After-tax return on a fully taxed interest stream is r_at = r_pt × (1 − t). After-tax volatility is σ_at = σ_pt × (1 − t) — the government shares the variance as well as the mean, because losses generate tax offsets that damp downside just as taxes clip upside. Worked: a bond sleeve with 4.6% pre-tax return and 7.0% volatility held by a 40% marginal-rate investor contributes 4.6 × 0.60 = 2.76% return and 7.0 × 0.60 = 4.20% volatility to the after-tax optimization. Feed PRE-tax numbers into a taxable investor's optimizer and the bond sleeve looks simultaneously too attractive on return and too risky on volatility — the errors do not cancel, they misshape the frontier.

Rebalancing thresholds widen with taxes: selling winners to rebalance triggers capital gains, so the cost of acting rises while the benefit (risk control) is unchanged. The rule: a taxable investor's optimal corridor is wider than a tax-exempt investor's for the same asset, and wider still for higher-rate investors and higher-unrealized-gain positions. Worked intuition: if the tax-exempt corridor on equities is ±5%, the taxable corridor might rationally sit at ±7–8%; the drift tolerance is buying tax deferral. Two further levers reduce the tax cost of staying near target: rebalance with CASH FLOWS first (direct new contributions and income toward underweights), and harvest losses — realizing losers to bank deductions that offset gains realized elsewhere, minding wash-sale-type rules and replacing exposure with a similar-but-not-identical instrument so the market position survives the tax trade.

Asset location is the allocation's sibling decision: place tax-INEFFICIENT assets (taxable bonds, REITs, high-turnover strategies) in tax-advantaged accounts, and tax-EFFICIENT assets (low-turnover equity, index exposure benefiting from deferred gains and favorable dividend treatment) in taxable accounts. The exam phrasing to retain: location decisions are made jointly with allocation — the investor holds one portfolio spread across wrappers, not a separate portfolio per account.

Deep Dive B — Revising the SAA: triggers taxonomy
A strategic allocation is revisited on a schedule and off-schedule when inputs genuinely change. The trigger families worth listing on an essay: (1) changes in GOALS — a liability materializes or disappears, the time horizon shortens, a business is sold; (2) changes in CONSTRAINTS — liquidity needs, tax status, regulation, size; (3) changes in BELIEFS — capital market expectations shift durably, not cyclically; and (4) changes in the investor's circumstances that alter risk capacity even with goals unchanged. The discriminator from TAA: strategic revision responds to durable changes in the investor or in long-run CME; tactical shifts respond to short-term expected-return dislocations with the SAA held constant. An exam answer that reallocates permanently because of a one-year view has confused the two.

Deep Dive C — Evaluating TAA
TAA earns its keep only if the sum of timing gains exceeds costs (trading, taxes, management attention) and the risk it adds. Evaluation tools: compare the realized Sharpe ratio of the portfolio WITH the tactical program against the SAA benchmark run passively; examine the hit rate of tactical calls and their payoff asymmetry; and regress realized excess returns on the tactical signals to test whether the process, not luck, drove results. Two structural cautions: TAA consumes part of the rebalancing benefit (it trades against the discipline of mechanical rebalancing), and in taxable accounts its hurdle rises by the tax cost of realization — a strategy that clears a tax-exempt hurdle can fail the same investor after tax.

Deep Dive D — Behavioral frictions in the allocation process, with remedies
Loss aversion: clients tolerate risk asymmetrically — the fix is framing risk in the currency of GOALS (probability of funding the liability) and, for fragile clients, goals-based structures that wall off required spending from risky growth assets. Illusion of control: excessive tactical tinkering and concentrated bets — the remedy is anchoring on the global market portfolio and requiring documented, testable views before deviating. Mental accounting: treating each account as its own portfolio — exploited constructively by goals-based allocation, but the aggregate must still be checked for coherence. Representativeness / recency: extrapolating recent winners — countered by disciplined rebalancing (which mechanically sells recent winners) and by long-horizon CME. Framing: risk tolerance answers depend on how questions are posed — mitigate with multiple framings and scenario-based questionnaires. Availability: overweighting vivid recent crises — mitigate with base-rate data on drawdown frequency and recovery. The essay-ready line: process is the antidote — a documented IPS, a rebalancing policy, and a governance calendar convert each bias from a decision into an exception that must be justified.

Additional self-test
9. A 45% bracket investor holds equities at +9% above target with large embedded gains. List three tax-aware paths back toward target.
   -> Direct new cash flows and income to underweights; widen the corridor (accept drift) rather than force sales; harvest available losses elsewhere to offset any gains realized.
10. Why does after-tax volatility fall with the tax rate?
   -> The tax authority shares gains AND losses; loss offsets damp downside, scaling volatility by (1 − t).
11. Classify: horizon shortens after a health event — strategic revision or TAA?
   -> Strategic revision: a durable change in investor circumstances, not a market view.
12. What three tests evaluate a TAA program?
   -> Sharpe ratio versus the passive SAA, hit rate/payoff asymmetry of calls, and attribution of excess return to the stated signals.
13. Where do high-turnover strategies belong in asset location, and why?
   -> Tax-advantaged accounts — their gains realize frequently and would be taxed at full rates in taxable wrappers.
14. Which bias does mechanical rebalancing directly counteract?
   -> Recency/representativeness (return chasing) — rebalancing systematically trims recent winners and adds to recent losers.

CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Portfolio Construction
Reading: Overview of Equity Portfolio Management
Orientation. A framing reading — no heavy math, but a lot of lists the exam likes to test as "describe" or "identify" prompts. Lock down four things: the roles equities play, the ways the universe is segmented, the income sources and costs of running an equity book (and the effect on net return), and the properties of a good benchmark plus the four index-weighting schemes and their tilts.

LOS 1 — The roles of equities in a portfolio
LOS: describe the roles of equities in the overall portfolio
What you must do:  List the functions equities serve and connect them to client objectives.
	•	Capital appreciation — the primary growth engine; equities have delivered the highest long-run returns among the major asset classes.
	•	Dividend income — a stream of cash returns, valuable for income-oriented clients and total return alike.
	•	Diversification — imperfect correlation with bonds and other assets improves the overall risk/return trade-off.
	•	Inflation hedge (long run) — companies can pass rising costs through to prices, so equities offer some real protection over long horizons (not reliably in the short run).
The appropriate role depends on the client: long-horizon, growth-oriented, risk-tolerant investors lean on equities heavily, while the equity weight also depends on the correlation of equities with the client's other holdings and liabilities.
Against these benefits, equities carry higher volatility and larger drawdowns than bonds, and they are more sensitive to the business cycle — so the equity weight is bounded by the client's risk tolerance, time horizon, and (for institutions) the need to fund liabilities.
Exam focus: A "describe the roles" list. Keep the four clean: growth, income, diversification, long-run inflation protection — and note that the short-run inflation hedge is weak.
LOS 2 — Segmenting the equity universe
LOS: describe how an equity manager’s investment universe can be segmented
What you must do:  Name the segmentation dimensions and the practical issues with classification.
Table 1 — Ways to segment the equity universe
Dimension
Segments
Size (market cap)
Large, mid, small, micro cap
Style
Value, growth, blend
Geography
Domestic, developed international, emerging, frontier
Economic sector / industry
GICS or ICB classification (e.g., 11 GICS sectors)
Size and style are often combined into a style box (small/mid/large × value/blend/growth). Segmentation defines the opportunity set, guides benchmark and manager selection, and supports diversification.
Watch out: There is no single universal classification: schemes differ (GICS vs ICB), some companies are hard to place (conglomerates, changing business mix), and indexes reconstitute periodically — so a stock's segment can change over time.
Exam focus: Know the four dimensions and the caveat that classification is not standardized. The size × style box is the most commonly referenced framework.
LOS 3 — Income and costs of owning an equity portfolio
LOS: describe the types of income and costs associated with owning and managing an equity portfolio and their potential effects on portfolio performance
What you must do:  Separate income sources from cost types and explain their drag on net return.
Table 2 — Income sources and cost types
Income sources
Cost types
Dividends
Management (and performance) fees
Securities-lending income (lending shares to short sellers for a fee)
Administration and custody costs
Dividend reinvestment / compounding
Explicit trading costs: commissions, taxes/stamp duty
—
Implicit trading costs: bid-ask spread, market impact
Effect on performance. Costs reduce net return directly, and high-turnover strategies incur far more trading cost, so turnover must be justified by added return. Securities-lending income can partly offset costs. Scale helps: larger portfolios spread fixed costs, though very large trades raise market impact.
Exam focus: Distinguish explicit costs (commissions, taxes — observable) from implicit costs (spread, market impact — estimated). Note securities lending as an income source that offsets costs, and that turnover is the main driver of trading cost.
LOS 4 — Shareholder engagement
LOS: describe the potential benefits of shareholder engagement and the role an equity manager might play in shareholder engagement
What you must do:  State the benefits and costs of engagement and the manager's channels for it.
What it is. Shareholder engagement is ongoing dialogue between investors and a company's management and board on strategy, capital allocation, governance, risk, remuneration, and ESG issues.
Benefits
	•	Can improve company performance and governance, and reduce risk.
	•	Better aligns management with shareholders' long-term interests.
	•	Gives the manager deeper insight into the company (an information benefit).
The manager's channels
	•	Voting proxies; direct dialogue and management meetings; filing or supporting shareholder proposals; and, for activists, public campaigns.
	•	Even passive/index managers engage — because they cannot sell an index constituent, stewardship is their primary lever to protect value.
Watch out: Engagement has costs: it consumes time and resources, can create conflicts of interest, may push short-term outcomes over long-term value, and suffers a free-rider problem (the benefits accrue to all shareholders, not just the engaging one).
Exam focus: A likely "describe the benefits/role" prompt. The high-value insight: passive managers must engage precisely because selling isn't an option.
LOS 5 — Rationales across the active-management spectrum
LOS: describe rationales for equity investment across the active management spectrum
What you must do:  Explain where passive, factor-based, and active sit on the spectrum and the rationale for each.
Table 3 — The active-management spectrum
Approach
Rationale
Passive (index)
Low cost and fees, tax efficiency, diversification, and a belief that markets are largely efficient — earns benchmark-like returns
Factor / smart beta / enhanced index
Capture rewarded factor premia (value, size, quality, momentum) at modest cost and tracking error
Active — fundamental
Discretionary judgment (top-down and/or bottom-up) to exploit mispricing and express views
Active — quantitative
Systematic, rules- and model-based signals to harvest inefficiencies at scale
Choosing a point on the spectrum depends on belief in market efficiency, the availability of manager skill NET of fees, cost sensitivity, tolerance for tracking error, and governance capacity to select and monitor active managers.
